content: DreamerV3 uses a Recurrent State-Space Model (RSSM) as its world model, where an encoder maps sensory inputs to discrete stochastic representations z_t, a GRU sequence model with recurrent state h_t predicts future representations given past actions, and the concatenation of h_t and z_t forms the model state for predicting rewards and reconstructing inputs.

DreamerV3 introduces the symlog transformation: symlog(x) = sign(x) * ln(|x| + 1), with inverse symexp(x) = sign(x) * (exp(|x|) - 1). This compresses magnitudes of large positive and negative targets symmetrically, enabling robust prediction of quantities with unknown scales (rewards, returns, observations) without introducing non-stationarity from running statistics normalization.

DreamerV3 uses symexp twohot encoding: the critic and reward predictor output softmax distributions over exponentially spaced bins B = symexp(-20...+20). Targets are twohot encoded as vectors with nonzero entries at the two closest bins summing to 1, enabling distributional prediction of arbitrary continuous values while decoupling gradient scale from target magnitude.

DreamerV3's world model loss combines prediction loss (L_pred for decoder, reward, continue), dynamics loss (L_dyn with KL divergence and stop-gradient on posterior), and representation loss (L_rep with KL divergence and stop-gradient on prior), with weights beta_pred=1, beta_dyn=1, beta_rep=0.1. Free bits clip dynamics and representation losses below 1 nat to prevent degenerate representations.

DreamerV3 normalizes returns using a percentile-based range S = EMA(Per(R,95) - Per(R,5), 0.99), dividing returns by max(1, S). This allows a fixed entropy scale eta=3e-4 across all domains, exploring rapidly under sparse rewards and converging well under dense rewards, unlike advantage normalization which amplifies noise under sparse rewards.

DreamerV3 is the first algorithm to collect diamonds in Minecraft from scratch without human data or curricula, using 100M environment steps on a single GPU over 9 days. All Dreamer agents trained discover diamonds, while competing baselines (PPO, IMPALA, Rainbow) fail to progress beyond the iron pickaxe.

Ablation studies show DreamerV3's performance predominantly rests on the unsupervised reconstruction loss of the world model rather than task-specific reward/value gradients. All robustness techniques contribute, most notably the KL objective, followed by return normalization and symexp twohot regression. Model scaling from 12M to 400M parameters increases both performance and data efficiency monotonically.

DreamerV3's critic is parameterized as a categorical distribution with exponentially spaced bins and trained via maximum likelihood on bootstrapped lambda-returns. It uses an EMA target network, zero-initialized output weights for the reward predictor and critic to prevent large initial predictions, and applies the critic loss to both imagined trajectories (scale 1) and replay buffer trajectories (scale 0.3).

DreamerV3 uses categorical distributions for encoder and dynamics predictor parameterized as mixtures of 1% uniform and 99% neural network output to prevent zero probabilities and ensure well-behaved KL losses, avoiding spikes observed in deep VAEs. The sequence model is a GRU with block-diagonal recurrent weights of 8 blocks.

DreamerV3 trains on a single A100 GPU but requires long training times (up to 16 GPU-days for ProcGen). The algorithm uses uniform replay rather than prioritized replay for simplicity. While it outperforms specialized methods across 150+ tasks with fixed hyperparameters, it has not yet been applied to real-world robotics at scale.

Genie is an 11B parameter generative interactive environment with three components: (1) a Latent Action Model (LAM) using VQ-VAE to learn discrete latent actions unsupervised from video, (2) a video tokenizer (ST-ViViT) compressing frames into discrete tokens via VQ-VAE, and (3) a MaskGIT-based dynamics model autoregressively predicting next frame tokens given past tokens and latent actions.

Genie's Latent Action Model learns a VQ codebook with |A|=8 discrete codes representing possible actions. The LAM encoder takes all previous frames plus next frame as input, and a decoder reconstructs the next frame using only history and the quantized latent action. The LAM is discarded at inference, replaced by user-provided actions. Latent actions are additive embeddings rather than token concatenations.

Genie uses a Spatiotemporal (ST) Transformer architecture across all model components, with interleaved spatial attention (within each frame's H*W tokens) and causal temporal attention (across T frames for same token position). The spatial attention cost scales linearly with frame count, making it much more efficient than full quadratic attention for long video sequences.

Genie's dynamics model is trained with random token masking (Bernoulli rate sampled uniformly between 0.5 and 1) during training. At inference, 25 MaskGIT steps with temperature 2 are used for frame sampling. bfloat16 and QK-norm are employed to stabilize large-scale training.

Genie demonstrates graceful scaling from 40M to 2.7B parameters, with each increase in model size yielding consistent decrease in final training loss. Increasing batch size from 128 to 448 (6.6M tokens) similarly improves performance. The final 10.1B dynamics model trained with batch size 512 for 125k steps on 256 TPUv5p achieves 942B tokens total.

Genie's learned latent actions transfer to unseen environments: using a frozen LAM to label expert videos with discrete actions and training a policy on CoinRun achieves the same score as oracle behavioral cloning with as few as 200 expert samples, despite never seeing CoinRun during training.

Genie's proposed ST-ViViT tokenizer outperforms both spatial-only ViT and temporal-spatial C-ViViT in FVD and controllability (ΔμPSNR). C-ViViT uses full space-time attention with significantly higher memory but exhibits overfitting. Pixel-input LAM achieves better controllability than token-input LAM (ΔμPSNR 1.91 vs 1.33 on Platformers).

Genie inherits weaknesses of autoregressive transformers including hallucinating unrealistic futures. It is limited to 16 frames of memory, making long-horizon consistency challenging. The model operates at approximately 1FPS, requiring future advances for efficient interactive frame rates.

Sora's architecture comprises three parts: (1) a time-space compressor mapping video into compact latent space, (2) a Diffusion Transformer (DiT) processing tokenized latent spacetime patches to denoise them, and (3) a CLIP-like conditioning mechanism receiving LLM-augmented text instructions. Videos are generated by iteratively denoising from Gaussian noise.

Sora trains on data at native sizes (variable durations, resolutions, aspect ratios) without resizing or cropping, enabled by patch n' pack (PNP) which packs multiple patches from different videos into single sequences with padding tokens. This significantly improves composition and framing compared to uniform square cropping.

Sora likely uses spatial-temporal VAE compression with 3D convolutions to jointly compress spatial and temporal video dimensions into latent space, followed by patchification into spacetime latent patches. This is analogous to word tokens in language models, providing Sora with detailed visual phrases to construct videos.

Sora follows a DALL-E 3-style caption improvement approach: training a video captioner to generate detailed descriptions, using these to re-caption training videos, and using GPT-4V to expand short user prompts to detailed descriptive prompts for consistency between training and inference.

Sora exhibits emergent abilities including 3D consistency with dynamic camera motion, long-range coherence with object persistence, and simulation of digital environments like Minecraft controlled by a basic policy. These emerge from scaling, not explicit 3D modeling, suggesting scale is effective for world simulation.

Sora likely leverages a cascade diffusion model architecture with a base model and spatial-temporal refiner models, using v-parameterization diffusion for superior performance. Temporal consistency is prioritized over spatial consistency, with an efficient strategy of using longer videos at lower resolution.

Sora's limitations include difficulty depicting complex actions and subtle facial expressions, physical simulation errors (objects appearing/disappearing, spatial inconsistencies), and ethical concerns about bias and harmful outputs. The model sometimes produces physically implausible results such as glass cups passing through each other.

DiT (Diffusion Transformer) incorporates conditioning via adaptive layer normalization (AdaLN) with zero-initializing MLP, which initializes each residual block as an identity function and stabilizes training. U-ViT treats all inputs as tokens and uses long skip connections between shallow and deep transformer layers.

TD-MPC2 learns an implicit (decoder-free) world model with five components: encoder h(s,e), latent dynamics d(z,a,e), reward predictor R(z,a,e), terminal value Q(z,a,e), and policy prior p(z,e). All components are MLPs with LayerNorm and Mish activations. The model is jointly optimized via joint-embedding prediction, reward prediction (cross-entropy), and TD-learning without decoding observations.

TD-MPC2 introduces SimNorm: projecting the latent representation z into L fixed-dimensional simplices using softmax with temperature tau. This naturally biases representations towards sparsity without hard constraints and is essential for training stability, mitigating exploding gradients. Reward and value prediction use discrete regression in a log-transformed space with cross-entropy loss.

TD-MPC2 uses Model Predictive Path Integral (MPPI) planning with horizon H, sampling action sequences from a time-dependent Gaussian N(mu, sigma^2), evaluating returns by rolling out latent trajectories, and updating parameters based on weighted average. The policy prior provides a fraction of initial samples to accelerate planning convergence, and warm-starting uses the previous step's solution shifted by 1.

TD-MPC2 uses an ensemble of 5 Q-functions with EMA targets (Q-bar), computing TD-targets as the minimum of two randomly sub-sampled Q-bar functions. The policy prior is a stochastic maximum entropy policy that maximizes alpha*Q - beta*H, with alpha tuned via moving statistics to prevent premature entropy collapse.

TD-MPC2 achieves strong results across 104 continuous control tasks spanning DMControl, Meta-World, ManiSkill2, and MyoSuite with a single set of hyperparameters, outperforming SAC, DreamerV3, and TD-MPC. It successfully trains a single 317M parameter agent to perform 80 tasks across multiple domains, embodiments, and action spaces, demonstrating that capabilities increase with model and data size.

TD-MPC2 handles multitask learning via learnable fixed-dimensional task embeddings e conditioned by all five model components, with L2-norm constrained to <= 1 for training stability and semantic coherence. Action masking zero-pads model inputs/outputs to largest dimensions and masks invalid action dimensions. This enables learning across multiple embodiments and action spaces without domain knowledge.

TD-MPC2 is limited to continuous control and local trajectory optimization (not globally optimal). Naively scaling TD-MPC (the predecessor) leads to decreased performance, which TD-MPC2's algorithmic improvements address. The approach relies on a UTD ratio of 1 by default, which is unusual compared to DreamerV3's UTD of 512.

DIAMOND (DIffusion As a Model Of eNvironment Dreams) uses a score-based diffusion model conditioned on past observations and actions as the world model. It adopts the EDM formulation with network preconditioning: D_theta = c_skip * x_noisy + c_out * F_theta(c_in * x_noisy, conditioning). The U-Net 2D architecture serves as the vector field F_theta, with actions injected via adaptive group normalization layers.

DIAMOND conditions the diffusion model on past observations by concatenating L past frames channel-wise to the noisy next observation, and inputs actions through adaptive group normalization layers in residual blocks. Euler's method is used for sampling (1 NFE per denoising step) as it is effective without the cost of higher-order samplers or stochastic sampling complexity.

DIAMOND uses separate reward and termination models (CNN + LSTM for partial observability) and trains an actor-critic network (shared CNN-LSTM with policy and value heads) using REINFORCE with value baseline and Bellman error with lambda-returns. The agent is trained entirely in imagination, only interacting with the real environment for data collection.

DIAMOND achieves a mean human normalized score of 1.46 on the Atari 100k benchmark (26 games, 2 hours of gameplay), a new state-of-the-art for agents trained entirely within a world model. It outperforms STORM, DreamerV3, IRIS, TWM, and SimPLe. Each run required approximately 2.9 days on a single RTX 4090 with 12GB VRAM.

DIAMOND's diffusion world model serves as an interactive neural game engine when trained on 87 hours of static Counter-Strike: Global Offensive (CSGO) gameplay, producing playable interactive simulations of the Dust II map. The improved performance on some Atari games follows from better modeling of critical visual details that discrete latent models lose.

DIAMOND's diffusion world model training objective: L(theta) = E[||F_theta(c_in * x_noisy, y) - (1/c_out)(x_clean - c_skip * x_noisy)||^2], where the network training target adaptively mixes signal and noise depending on noise level sigma(tau). At high noise, target is the clean signal; at low noise, target is the difference between clean and perturbed signal.

DIAMOND's diffusion-based world model is computationally more expensive per imagination step than discrete latent models due to the iterative denoising process, though this is mitigated by using few denoising steps (Euler sampling). The approach has been demonstrated only on Atari-scale environments, not yet on higher-resolution or more complex domains.

Cosmos WFM Platform explores two scalable approaches: (1) transformer-based diffusion models using continuous tokens (Text2World then Video2World pre-training), and (2) transformer-based autoregressive models using discrete tokens (vanilla next-token then text-conditioned Video2World). Both use causal video tokenizers that process current frames without future information.

Cosmos develops a video data curation pipeline with 5 steps: (1) Splitting via TransNetV2 shot detection, (2) Filtering (motion, visual quality, text overlay, video type), (3) Annotation using VILA 13B VLM for captions, (4) Semantic deduplication via InternVideo2 embeddings and k-means clustering, removing ~30% of data, (5) Sharding by resolution and aspect ratio.

Cosmos processes 20M hours of raw video down to ~100M clips of 2-60 seconds for pre-training and ~10M clips for fine-tuning. The pipeline categorizes videos into 9 types (driving 11%, hand motion 16%, human motion 10%, spatial navigation 16%, nature dynamics 20%, etc.) and upsamples task-relevant categories while downsampling less important ones.

Cosmos develops attention-based encoder-decoder video tokenizers for both continuous and discrete tokens. Causal design enables joint image-video training since a causal video tokenizer becomes an image tokenizer for single images. Multiple compression ratios are explored. Tokenizers are key to scaling WFM training as computation grows with token counts.

Cosmos post-training includes camera control (camera pose conditioning for navigation), robotic manipulation (fine-tuning on action-video pairs for humanoid and manipulation tasks), and autonomous driving. A diffusion decoder is fine-tuned to enhance autoregressive model outputs. An LLM-based prompt upsampler improves control, and a guardrail system blocks harmful inputs/outputs.

Cosmos positions WFM use cases as: policy evaluation (testing policies in simulated worlds), policy initialization (WFM as good policy model initialization), policy training (WFM + reward model as RL environment), planning/MPC (simulating different futures for action selection), and synthetic data generation (including Sim2Real conditioned on depth/semantic maps).

Cosmos achieves GPU transcoding throughput of 0.3702 videos/s using PyNvideoCodec on L40S GPUs with h264_nvenc codec (batch=1, 3 NVDEC, 3 NVENC accelerators), representing a 6.5x improvement over baseline ffmpeg configurations. Hardware-accelerated transcoding is critical for processing 20M hours of video data.

This survey categorizes world models into two primary functions: (1) constructing implicit representations to understand world mechanisms (model-based RL, LLM world knowledge), and (2) predicting future states to simulate and guide decision-making (video generation, interactive 3D environments). This taxonomy spans from Ha & Schmidhuber's RWM (2018) through Dreamer, JEPA, Sora, and Cosmos.

JEPA (Joint Embedding Predictive Architecture) by LeCun predicts abstract representations in latent space rather than raw pixels, capturing semantic relationships and causal structures while avoiding computational burden of pixel-level prediction. It incorporates dual-system thinking: System 1 (intuitive fast reactions) and System 2 (deliberate reasoning using world model). Implementations include V-JEPA and V-JEPA2.

World model learning in MBRL uses either deterministic transition models (minimizing MSE on state predictions) or probabilistic models (minimizing KL divergence between transition distributions). Policy generation uses MPC (planning action sequences to maximize expected reward over horizon) or MCTS (maintaining search trees with value function evaluation at each node).

LLMs as world models: LLMs trained on vast corpora naturally acquire latent world knowledge including spatial/temporal understanding. MLLMs process multimodal information creating richer world representations. Applications include direct action generation, modular usage with external planners, and web navigation via world-model-augmented agents that predict action outcomes.

Video generation models (Sora, Keling, Gen-2) represent world modeling by explicitly generating visual sequences demonstrating understanding of temporal dynamics, spatial consistency, and physical laws. These models maintain 3D consistency, produce physically plausible outcomes, and simulate complex environments, suggesting they model underlying real-world dynamics rather than merely generating visually appealing content.

World model applications span four key domains: generative games (WHAM), autonomous driving (GAIA-1, Drive-WM for traffic modeling), robotics (DayDreamer for locomotion/manipulation, GR1/GR2), and social simulacra (generative agents modeling societal dynamics). Each domain emphasizes different aspects of understanding vs. prediction.

DrivingWorld is a GPT-style autoregressive world model for autonomous driving with a hybrid token prediction: (1) next-state prediction at the frame level via a Temporal-multimodal Fusion Module (causal temporal attention + bidirectional multimodal attention), and (2) next-token prediction within each state via an Internal-state Autoregressive Module. This decouples temporal and spatial modeling.

DrivingWorld proposes a temporal-aware VQ tokenizer that inserts self-attention layers along the temporal dimension both before and after VQGAN quantization, capturing long-term temporal relationships between frames. The model builds on LlamaGen's VQGAN with codebook size 16,384 and 70M parameters, trained with charbonnier loss, LPIPS perceptual loss, and codebook loss.

DrivingWorld uses a vehicle pose tokenizer with relative ego-centric poses (orientation discretized into alpha bins, location into beta*gamma bins) to avoid magnitude growth issues of global poses. Token dropout (50% chance replacing tokens with random tokens in 30% of conditioning sequences) mitigates autoregressive drifting. Balanced attention adds constant weights (0.4 for orientation, 0.2 for location) before softmax.

DrivingWorld generates 40+ second videos at 10Hz (640 frames from 15 conditioning frames) with 1B parameters, trained on 3456+ hours of human driving data. It achieves comparable FVD to Vista on NuScenes (zero-shot) despite being trained from scratch rather than on pre-trained Stable Video Diffusion. QK-norm and 2D-rotary positional encoding stabilize training.

DrivingWorld's autoregressive approach has computational cost growing with sequence length (each frame generates 512 image tokens + 2 pose tokens). The 1D causal attention in the internal-state module has quadratic cost with tokens. Generating very long videos requires progressively more computation as context grows.

RLVR-World unifies world modeling across modalities (text, video, proprioceptive) as autoregressive sequence modeling: current states and actions are encoded as question tokens, next states as response tokens. Post-training uses GRPO (Group Relative Policy Optimization) which eliminates value functions and estimates advantages in a group-relative manner with clipped objective and KL divergence penalty.

RLVR-World computes verifiable rewards as R_i = sign(D) * D(s_hat_i, s'), where D is a distance metric between predicted and ground-truth next states. For language: accuracy or F1 score on state prediction. For video: MSE, LPIPS, or SSIM on decoded predicted frames. sign(D)=-1 for metrics where lower is better (MSE, LPIPS).

On language world models, RLVR-World with DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Qwen-1.5B achieves +30.7% accuracy improvement on text game state prediction (ByteSized32) and +15.1% F1 on web page state prediction (WebArena) over SFT. The 7B model with RLVR achieves overall accuracy of 65.53%, surpassing GPT-4's 64.76% on text game state prediction.

On video world models (robot manipulation trajectory prediction on RT-1), RLVR-World achieves +9.2% relative improvement on LPIPS with merely a few hundred RLVR gradient steps, compared to hundreds of thousands required by MLE training. It also reduces repetition rate from 48.6% to 9.9% for multi-step prediction, demonstrating RLVR mitigates the repetition issue common in autoregressive models.

RLVR-World uses compressive visual tokenization where each video frame is tokenized into n reduced tokens conditioned on N shared context tokens (K1 and K2 codebooks), exploiting temporal redundancy in videos. This reduces token sequence length compared to independent per-frame tokenization.

RLVR-World is a post-training paradigm requiring a pre-trained world model via MLE as starting point. The approach is demonstrated on relatively small-scale models (1.5B-7B for language, undisclosed for video). The effectiveness of RLVR may depend on the quality and coverage of the reward metrics chosen.

RoboScape is a physics-informed embodied world model using a Dual-branch Co-autoregressive Transformer (DCT) with parallel RGB and depth branches, each consisting of stacked Spatial-Temporal Transformer blocks. Causal attention in temporal layers, bidirectional in spatial layers. Cross-branch interaction: depth features are projected via learnable linear layer W^l and additively fused into RGB features at each ST-Transformer block.

RoboScape introduces adaptive keypoint dynamics learning: SpatialTracker densely samples N0 keypoints, then top-K most active keypoints are selected based on cumulative motion magnitude. Temporal consistency loss enforces alignment of keypoint-located predicted tokens across frames: L_keypoint = (1/((T-1)K)) * sum ||s_hat_t(p_i) - s_hat_1(p_i)||^2. This implicitly encodes material properties through self-supervised keypoint tracking.

RoboScape uses MAGVIT-2 for video tokenization, compressing RGB frames and depth maps into discrete latent tokens with spatial downsampling factor alpha. Physics-informed joint training objective: L = L_RGB + lambda1*L_Depth + lambda2*L_Keypoint + lambda3*L_Attention, where L_Attention uses a keypoint-guided attention map with importance weight gamma=5 for keypoint regions.

RoboScape outperforms IRASim, iVideoGPT, Genie, and CogVideoX across six metrics (LPIPS, PSNR, AbsRel, delta1, delta2, ΔPSNR). Ablation shows depth learning preserves geometric consistency and keypoint learning is essential for visual fidelity and action controllability. Removing both drops ΔPSNR from 3.34 to 1.99, confirming physics injection's value.

RoboScape's synthetic data improves Diffusion Policy success rate from 40% (50 clips) to 91% (200 clips) on Robomimic, matching real data (92%). For π0 on LIBERO, 800 synthetic clips achieve 79.1% average success vs 65.2% with 200 real clips. The model also serves as a reliable policy evaluator with strong correlation to ground-truth simulator outcomes.

RoboScape constructs a data processing pipeline from AGIBOT-World dataset using Video Depth Anything for depth generation, SpatialTracker for keypoint tracking, TransNetV2 for clip slicing, FlowNet for motion filtering, and Intern-VL for action semantic labeling. Clips are categorized by action difficulty (3 levels: pick/push, place/turn, wipe/fold) and scenes for curriculum learning.

RoboScape's physics-informed approach is validated on robot manipulation scenarios from AGIBOT-World. The depth and keypoint annotations rely on off-the-shelf pretrained models, which may introduce errors. The approach has been demonstrated on 16-frame clips at 2Hz, and scaling to longer horizons or higher resolutions is untested.

World-in-World introduces a unified closed-loop online planning strategy with three phases: (1) Proposal - sampling M candidate action plans from a proposal policy, (2) Simulation - world model rollouts predicting future states for each plan, (3) Revision - a revision policy scores and selects the best plan. This forms a policy-guided beam search executed repeatedly in a closed loop with the environment.

World-in-World proposes a unified Action API that transforms action sequences into three control types: (1) text prompts (for text-to-video models), (2) camera trajectories/viewpoints (translation by 0.2m, rotation by 22.5 degrees), and (3) low-level discrete/continuous actions mapped to model vocabularies. This enables heterogeneous WMs to be evaluated under a consistent interface.

World-in-World's key finding: high visual quality does NOT guarantee task success. Among 14 models benchmarked, the correlation between generation quality (aesthetic+image quality) and task success rate is weak. Post-trained models (e.g., SVD† at 0.325 quality achieves 62.98% success) outperform higher-quality zero-shot models (e.g., Wan2.1 at 0.475 quality achieves 58.26%). Controllability matters more than visual fidelity.

World-in-World shows scaling post-training with action-observation data is more effective than upgrading pretrained video generators. Post-trained SVD† outperforms zero-shot Wan2.2 A14B on Active Recognition (62.98% vs 59.53%). Inference-time scaling via more online planning rollouts substantially improves closed-loop performance across all tasks.

World-in-World evaluates four embodied tasks: Active Recognition (identifying occluded targets, Matterport3D), Active Embodied QA (answering questions after exploration, OpenEQA), Image-Goal Navigation (reaching viewpoints matching goal images, HM3D), and Robotic Manipulation (7-DoF gripper tasks, RLBench). Tasks span perception, navigation, and manipulation.

World-in-World's post-training procedure fine-tunes pretrained video generators on action-observation datasets from Habitat-Sim (panoramic) and CoppeliaSim/RLBench, using scenes disjoint from evaluation to test generalization. This aligns the model with domain distribution and action space of target environments.

World-in-World is limited to relatively simple simulated environments (Habitat-Sim, RLBench). The evaluation does not cover open-world or real-robot settings. The closed-loop planning requires multiple world model rollouts per decision step, creating computational overhead proportional to the number of candidate plans and rollout horizon.

Infinite-World introduces a Hierarchical Pose-free Memory Compressor (HPMC) with two modes: (1) Direct Compression for short sequences (L <= k*T_max) using a temporal encoder reducing resolution by factor k=4, and (2) Hierarchical Compression for long sequences via overlapping sliding-window chunks, first-stage local compression, then second-stage global compression. The 3D-ResNet compressor is jointly optimized with the DiT backbone end-to-end.

Infinite-World proposes uncertainty-aware action labeling with tri-state logic: No-operation (||ΔP|| < τ1), Discrete Action (||ΔP|| > τ2), Uncertain (otherwise). Motion is decoupled into translation {W,A,S,D} and rotation {Left,Right,Up,Down}. The 'Uncertain' state retains low-SNR motion data, maximizing raw video utilization while shielding action labels from noisy jitter. Action embeddings use 1D-conv layers with stride 2 for 4x downsampling matching compressed history resolution.

Infinite-World uses a Revisit-Dense Finetuning Strategy: pre-train on large-scale open-domain internet video (30+ hours) for visual priors, then fine-tune on a compact 30-minute Revisit-Dense Dataset (RDD) of self-captured iPhone footage with frequent loop-closures. A pilot study shows memory activation saturates at ~100 training sequences, and exceeding the training temporal window causes catastrophic memory collapse.

Infinite-World achieves coherent interactive world simulation over 1000+ frames, with context of up to 320 frames recursively compressed into fixed memory budget T_max=20. It uses N=5 overlapping chunks with window size W=64 frames. Built on Wan2.1-1.3B, trained at 480P resolution with AdamW optimizer (lr=1e-5) on 16 NVIDIA H800 GPUs. Outperforms HY-World-1.5, HunyuanGameCraft, Yume 1.5, and Matrix-Game 2.0 in user study (ELO 1719 vs next best 1542).

Infinite-World identifies three core challenges in real-world interactive world modeling: (1) inaccurate pose estimation from raw video making precise action-response mapping difficult, (2) scarcity of viewpoint revisit data in natural video preventing global geometric learning, and (3) lack of efficient pose-free memory mechanisms with O(L^2) attention complexity creating computational bottlenecks for long videos.

Infinite-World's pilot study reveals that memory acquisition depends on topological variety of trajectories rather than data quantity: 10-50 sequences enable basic referencing, 100 sequences establish robust spatial memory, and 1000 sequences yield marginal gains. The bottleneck is duration and topological density of individual trajectories, not total data volume.

Infinite-World is limited to keyboard-style discrete controls (WASD + rotations) rather than continuous camera control. The RDD fine-tuning dataset is self-captured with an iPhone 17 Pro in Action Mode. While achieving 1000+ frame consistency, the model has been evaluated on 100 benchmark scenarios and may not generalize to all environment types.

The world model survey traces intellectual roots to Kenneth Craik's mental models theory (1943) and Johnson-Laird's Mental Models Theory (1983), which posit that human cognition constructs internal representations enabling predictive simulation. These principles—finite representations of infinite domains, structural relationship capture, and predictive scenario simulation—directly parallel modern AI world models.

The survey identifies open challenges for world models including: scaling to longer temporal horizons, improving sample efficiency, handling multi-modal inputs robustly, ensuring physical plausibility of generated futures, bridging the gap between understanding and prediction, and developing standardized evaluation protocols that go beyond visual quality to measure decision-making utility.

DrivingWorld outperforms GAIA-1, DriveDreamer, Drive-WM, WoVoGen, and Vista in FVD on NuScenes despite training from scratch (vs prior methods based on pre-trained Stable Video Diffusion). Without private data, DrivingWorld (w/o P) still achieves competitive FVD of 15.8, comparable to Drive-WM's 15.8 and Vista's comparable performance, but generates significantly longer videos (64s vs 8-16s).

DrivingWorld's world model training uses cross-entropy loss over the joint distribution of next-state tokens (pose + image): L_WM = -sum log P(r_hat | r_<t, r^1_t, ..., r^{j-1}_t). Two teacher-forcing strategies are applied: one at the frame level (next-state prediction) and one at the internal-state level (next-token prediction within a frame).

RLVR-World demonstrates downstream utility: reinforced language world models enable model predictive control for web agents, improving task success rate from 12.06% (SFT baseline) to 14.29% (+18.4%) on WebArena. The reinforced video world models also serve as effective policy evaluators, bridging the gap between pre-trained models and target world modeling tasks.

RLVR-World formulates world modeling as a new MDP distinct from the original environment: the 'state' is the current world state and action, the 'policy' is the world model generating next states, and the 'reward' is the verifiable prediction metric. This reframing allows standard RL algorithms like GRPO to optimize world model quality directly.

TD-MPC2's ablation studies show that SimNorm is essential for training stability, preventing exploding gradients that caused TD-MPC to diverge on high-dimensional tasks like Dog locomotion (A ∈ R38). The 5M parameter TD-MPC2 agent uses a UTD ratio of 1, far lower than DreamerV3's 512, and a batch size of 256 vs SAC/TD-MPC's 512.

DIAMOND's EDM-based noise schedule samples sigma from a log-normal distribution to concentrate training around medium-noise regions, preventing high variance at extremes. The training objective's preconditioners c_skip, c_out, c_in maintain unit variance for network input/output across all noise levels. This approach proved more effective than DDPM for world modeling.

Cosmos develops a guardrail system with a pre-Guard (blocking harmful inputs) and post-Guard (blocking harmful outputs) to ensure safe usage of world foundation models. The open-weight release under NVIDIA Open Model License includes pre-trained WFMs and tokenizers, with 300+ model checkpoints released publicly.

DrivingWorld's internal-state autoregressive module generates next-state tokens autoregressively: starting from [sos], each predicted token r_hat is added with the temporal-multimodal output feature, then processed through causal-attention transformer layers. This two-level teacher forcing (frame-level + token-level) captures both inter-frame temporal dependencies and intra-frame spatial structure.

RoboScape uses 50,000 video clips from AGIBOT-World covering 147 tasks and 72 skills, processed into 16-frame clips at 2Hz (6.5M training clips). Training uses 5 epochs with lambda1=1, lambda2=0.01, lambda3=1, gamma=5, completing in 24 hours on 32 NVIDIA A800-80GB GPUs. Inference uses first frame as condition for autoregressive prediction of 15 subsequent frames.

World-in-World benchmarks 14 diverse world models including SVD, LTX-Video, Hunyuan, Wan2.1, Wan2.2, Cosmos-P2, NWM, PathDreamer, SE3DS, and Runway Gen4 across four tasks. The best proprietary model (Runway Gen4) achieves 64.79% on Active Recognition vs 50.27% base VLM policy, demonstrating WMs consistently improve base policies across all tasks.

Infinite-World's context injection to DiT uses temporal concatenation of compressed history z_com, last-frame latent z_loc (local memory), and noisy target latents z_t, with a binary mask m distinguishing context from denoising target. Action embeddings are element-wise added to video tokens after patching, enabling temporally-aligned action modulation with minimal overhead.

Ctrl-World is a controllable multi-view generative world model for robot manipulation, initialized from the 1.5B Stable Video Diffusion (SVD) pretrained checkpoint. It enables policy-in-the-loop rollouts entirely within imagination space, supporting closed-loop interaction between VLA policies (π0, π0-FAST, π0.5) and the world model for both evaluation and synthetic data generation for fine-tuning.

Multi-view joint prediction: concatenates N camera views (each H×W tokens) along the token dimension to jointly predict all views ot:t+H. Uses spatial-temporal transformer backbone. Trained to jointly predict outputs from 3 cameras (2 third-person + 1 wrist) at 192×320 resolution. The wrist-camera prediction significantly reduces hallucinations during contact-rich object interactions by providing fine-grained contact information.

Pose-conditioned memory retrieval: samples k=7 history frames with stride m=1-2 seconds, adds them to context, and embeds corresponding robot arm poses [qt-km,...,qt] into frames via frame-wise cross-attention within the spatial transformer. This anchors future predictions to relevant history, preventing prediction drift and incoherence over long rollouts. Attention visualization confirms strong attention to frames with similar poses when predicting t=4s frames.

Frame-level action conditioning: transforms each action sequence [at+1:t+H] into Cartesian-space robot arm poses [a't+1:t+H], concatenates with past poses, and applies frame-wise cross-attention (following IRASim approach) within the spatial transformer so each frame's visual tokens attend to its associated pose embedding. Achieves centimeter-level control precision. 15 future actions (1-second chunk) are temporally downsampled to 5 steps for GPU efficiency. The only newly initialized parameter is a 3-layer MLP projecting 7D Cartesian actions to 1024D embeddings.

Training objective uses diffusion loss: L = E[||x̂0(x_t', t', c) - x0||²] where the prediction target x0=ot+1:t+H is perturbed with Gaussian noise ε~N(0,I) at diffusion step t'∈[0,T] with scheduler αt', giving xt'=αt'·x0 + √(1-αt')·εt'. Model input is concatenation [ot-km,...,ot-m,ot,xt'] of history tokens and noised future. Only the action-projection MLP is newly initialized; all other SVD parameters are kept at initialization.

World model quality: On DROID validation set (256 clips, 10s trajectories, 10 autoregressive rounds), Ctrl-World achieves FVD=97.4 (third-view) and 127.1 (wrist-view), outperforming WPE (156.4/165.4) and IRASim (138.1). Ablations show removing memory increases FVD to 105.5/133.1, removing frame-level conditioning increases to 122.7/179.1, and removing joint multi-view prediction degrades wrist-view FVD to 158.1.

Policy evaluation correlation: World model instruction-following rates correlate strongly with real-world rates (y=0.87x-0.04), while execution success rates show weaker correlation (y=0.81x-0.11) due to imprecise modeling of complex physics dynamics (collisions, sliding, rotations). The model tends to underestimate execution success rate. Generalist policies transfer zero-shot to new DROID setups with novel camera placements.

Policy improvement via synthetic data: Using Algorithm 1, generates 400 trajectories per task with diversity via instruction rephrasing (LLM-based paraphrasing) and random initial state resets. Retains 25-50 successful trajectories based on human preference. Fine-tuning π0.5 for 2k steps on curated synthetic data improves average success rate from 38.7% to 83.4% (44.7% improvement) across spatial understanding, shape understanding, towel-folding direction, and novel object tasks.

Joint-space to Cartesian-space adapter: VLA policies output joint velocities, but the world model conditions on end-effector poses in Cartesian space. A two-layer MLP adapter maps current joint angles qt_joint and predicted joint velocities ajv to future joint configurations, which are then converted via Franka Panda forward kinematics (FK) to Cartesian poses. This enables seamless autoregressive interaction between open-source policies and Ctrl-World.

Limitations: Model fails on tasks requiring precise interactions or long-horizon reasoning; performance is sensitive to initial observations. Cannot accurately model complex physics dynamics like collisions, object sliding, and rotations. Does not capture policies' retrying behavior after failed attempts. Many failure modes remain outside the DROID data distribution. Not accurate enough to improve low-level success rates on previously seen instructions.

ResWorld (Temporal Residual World Model / TR-World) for end-to-end autonomous driving. Instead of modeling the entire scene, it focuses on dynamic objects by computing temporal residuals of BEV features. Static objects are directly retained from current BEV features (same coordinate system), avoiding redundant modeling. Combined with Future-Guided Trajectory Refinement (FGTR) for explicit trajectory interaction with predicted future BEV features.

Temporal residual extraction: BEV features {Bt, Bt-1,...,Bt-k} from k=2 past timestamps are transformed into the coordinate system of Bt (following BEVDet4D). Fused via Bfuse=Conv(Concat(Bt,...,Bt-k)). TokenLearner generates spatial attention map from fused features, extracting sparse scene queries Si=AvgPool(SA(Bfuse)⊙Bi). Temporal residuals Ri=St-St-1 capture changes at same positions across timestamps, representing dynamic objects without detection/tracking.

TR-World architecture: Each temporal residual Ri undergoes self-attention, then accumulates across timestamps: R̂=Σ SelfAttention(Ri). Future BEV features computed via TokenFuser: Bfuture = TokenFuser(R̂, Bfuse) + Bfuse = MLP(Bfuse)⊗R̂ + Bfuse. This maps predicted dynamic object distribution onto current BEV features, producing accurate future BEV predictions. Static objects need no modeling since coordinate system is preserved.

Future-Guided Trajectory Refinement (FGTR): Uses waypoint queries W∈R^{Nt×C} for ego vehicle future status. Prior trajectory Tprior decoded via MLP from CrossAttention(W, Sfuse). FGTR applies DeformAttention(W, Bfuture, Tprior) where Tprior serves as reference points on Bfuture. This checks for collisions/drivable area violations and corrects Tprior, while providing sparse spatial-temporal supervision on Bfuture to prevent world model collapse.

Key insight on NOT supervising Bfuture: Supervising future BEV features with ground truth at any single future timestamp loses spatial distribution of dynamic objects at other timestamps. Not applying supervision allows the model to independently optimize future BEV features and retain the most important cross-temporal information. Experiments confirm TR-World without future supervision achieves 0.59 avg L2 vs 0.61 with supervision; collision rate 0.17 vs 0.21.

nuScenes results (open-loop): ResWorld with ego status achieves SOTA: L2 avg=0.59m (1s: 0.19, 2s: 0.50, 3s: 1.08), collision rate avg=0.17% (1s: 0.02, 2s: 0.06, 3s: 0.43). Outperforms SSR (0.74/0.31), GenAD (0.65/0.23), DiffusionDrive (0.57/0.08), BEVPlanner++ (0.35/0.34). Uses no auxiliary perception tasks, fully relying on world model for scene understanding.

NAVSIM results (closed-loop): ResWorld with historical frame data achieves 89.0% PDMS (No Collision: 98.9, Drivable Area: 96.5, TTC: 95.6, Comfort: 100, Ego Progress: 83.1), outperforming Hydra-MDP (86.5) and DiffusionDrive (88.1). Without auxiliary tasks, achieves 87.3% PDMS, outperforming LAW (84.6) and World4Drive (85.1). Temporal residuals effectively represent dynamic information in driving scenes.

Ablation: TR-World+FGTR together reduce 8.4% of baseline avg L2 error and 25.8% of avg collision rate without ego status; with ego status, 9.2% L2 reduction and 39.3% collision rate reduction. Prior trajectory (same baseline architecture) already achieves significant improvement (0.61 vs 0.65 avg L2) because BEV features are optimized by TR-World+FGTR modules during training.

Training: Uses GeoBEV for high geometric quality BEV features (replacing BEVFormer in SSR). ResNet-50 backbone, multi-view images at 256×704. AdamW optimizer, lr=1e-4, trained 12 epochs on 8× RTX 3090 GPUs, batch size 8. For NAVSIM: TransFuser-like structure with ResNet-34 backbones, 100 epochs, batch size 512, lr=6e-4. Only L1 loss on Tprior and Tfinal is used for training.

Limitation: TR-World cannot adequately capture potential dynamic objects (pedestrians, parked cars) through temporal residuals alone—objects that haven't moved yet but may move. Such objects are only processed alongside static objects by the prior trajectory prediction branch. Future work: use coarse perception to extract information of potential dynamic objects and perform preventive modeling for improved safety.

DreamDojo is a foundation world model for dexterous robot tasks pretrained on 44k hours of egocentric human videos (DreamDojo-HV), the largest video dataset to date for world model pretraining. Built on Cosmos-Predict2.5 (latent video diffusion with WAN2.2 tokenizer). Uses continuous latent actions as unified proxy actions for all videos. Post-trained on target robot data (GR-1, G1, AgiBot, YAM) for action space adaptation.

Continuous latent action model: VAE with information bottleneck design using spatiotemporal Transformer architecture. Encoder takes two consecutive frames (xt:xt+1), extracts spatiotemporal features, projects to 32-dimensional embedding ât. Decoder receives embedding + former frame to predict subsequent frame. Supervised by reconstruction loss + KL divergence with β=1e-6. Compactness + regularization create information bottleneck forcing disentanglement of critical motion information. Latent dimension=32.

Architecture improvements: (1) Relative actions: transforms absolute joint poses to relative by rebaselining with pose at beginning of each latent frame (every 4 timesteps), reducing modeling complexity. (2) Chunked action injection: concatenates 4 consecutive actions as chunk per latent frame (matching WAN2.2 temporal compression ratio of 4), injecting causality prior that future actions don't aid current predictions. Actions projected via MLP with zero-initialized last layer to avoid perturbing pretrained model.

Temporal consistency loss: Beyond standard flow matching loss Lflow, adds Ltemporal = E[||Σ(z_{k+1}-z_k) - (v_{k+1}-v_k)||²] to match ground-truth temporal transitions between predicted velocity frames z_k and ground-truth velocity v_k. Final objective: Lfinal = Lflow + λ·Ltemporal with λ=0.1. This loss accelerates action controllability learning and enhances object completeness while reducing artifacts.

Distillation pipeline (Self-Forcing paradigm): Two-stage distillation from bidirectional teacher (35 denoising steps) to causal student (4 steps). (1) Warmup: student regresses to teacher ODE solutions with teacher forcing. (2) Distillation: student uses own generated latents as context, supervised via KL divergence distribution matching loss (DMD). Student generates L'>L frames during training to simulate longer rollouts. Achieves 10.81 FPS (4× speedup) at 640×480 resolution. Causal attention replaces bidirectional over sliding window of 12 frames.

Latent action efficacy (Table 2): On In-lab Eval, latent action conditioning (PSNR=20.913) matches retargeted action (PSNR=20.960) and outperforms action-free pretraining (20.797) and no-pretraining (20.576). On EgoDex Eval, latent action (PSNR=20.344) outperforms MANO (20.474 not comparable due to ideal capture) and action-free (19.924). Latent actions significantly narrow the gap to ground-truth action labels while being fully self-supervised and scalable.

Data mixture effects (Table 3): Adding datasets progressively improves performance. In-lab only → In-lab+EgoDex → full mixture (In-lab+EgoDex+DreamDojo-HV): PSNR improves from 20.913→21.016 (In-lab), 20.267→20.414 (EgoDex), 18.621→18.724 (DreamDojo-HV), 20.755→20.852 (Counterfactual). DreamDojo-14B achieves best: PSNR 21.413/20.525/18.924/21.087 across benchmarks. Human evaluation: DreamDojo-14B wins 63.45% physics + 72.55% action following vs Cosmos-Predict2.5.

Policy evaluation: On AgiBot fruit packing (20 scenes, 5 fruits=100% success), DreamDojo-2B post-trained on AgiBot dataset shows Pearson r=0.995 and MMRV=0.003 between real-world and simulator success rates. Model-based planning: ensembles 5 policy checkpoints, DreamDojo predicts futures, external DINOv2-based value model selects best proposal. For high-variance policy group: 17% improvement over best checkpoint, ~2× success rate vs uniform sampling.

Distillation results (Table 6): On GR-1 Long Eval (600 frames = 1 minute), teacher achieves PSNR=14.086 at 2.72 FPS vs student PSNR=13.146 at 10.81 FPS. Student uses predict_len=4 (vs teacher 12) and context_len=12 (vs teacher 1). Student's autoregressive causal prediction enables real-time streaming and better context awareness. Pretraining benefits preserved after distillation: student with pretraining consistently outperforms student without across all 4 benchmarks.

DreamDojo-HV dataset: 43,827 hours from crowdsourcing spanning household, industrial, retail, educational, administrative environments. Contains ~9,869 unique scenes, 6,015 unique tasks, 43,237 unique objects. Represents 15× longer duration, 96× more skills, 2000× more scenes than previously largest dataset for world model training. Total mixture: 44,711 hours (In-lab: 55h, EgoDex: 829h, DreamDojo-HV: 43,827h). Sampling ratio 1:2:10 for pretraining.

Limitations: Model imperfect when simulating uncommon actions (slapping, fast waving). Policy evaluation absolute success rates are often higher than real counterparts, indicating limited ability to generate nuanced failures. Does not naturally support multi-view simulation. Knowledge retention during post-training has not been studied in depth—future work could explore fine-tuning strategies like LoRA for better target embodiment adaptation.

Γ-World (Gamma-World) is a generative multi-agent world model extending beyond two players for interactive simulation. Built on Cosmos-Predict2.5-2B DiT with flow-matching objective. Addresses three requirements for multi-agent world modeling: independent controllability, permutation symmetry, and efficient inference. Key innovations: Simplex Rotary Agent Encoding (parameter-free agent identity) and Sparse Hub Attention (linear cross-agent cost). Distills bidirectional teacher into causal student for 24 FPS streaming.

Simplex Rotary Agent Encoding: Extends 3D RoPE with explicit agent axis (4D rotary operator). Agents represented as vertices of regular simplex in rotary angle space: s_κ = √(κ/(κ-1))·Q(e_κ - 1/κ·1), with pairwise equidistance ||s_κ-s_κ'||² = 2κ/(κ-1). Agent angles θ_d = α·s_λ(d) with scale α>0. Parameter-free, permutation-symmetric, does not rely on learned slot identities. Simplex pool size κ=4 over 2 active runtime slots; head dimensions partitioned as (64,32,16,16) across (t,d,h,w).

Sparse Hub Attention (SHA): Learnable hub tokens mediate cross-agent interaction. Agent tokens attend only to own stream + hub tokens; hub tokens attend to all agents + other hubs. Cross-agent info flows through two-hop path: agent→hub→agent. Reduces attention cost from O(P²·b²·L²) to O(P·bL(bL+bH)), linear in number of agents P. Topology: M_hub(k,j)=1[μ(k)=μ(j) ∨ μ(k)=hub ∨ μ(j)=hub], composed with block-causal mask. Uses H=8 learnable hub tokens per latent frame.

Three-stage training recipe: (1) Bidirectional teacher: full multi-agent sequence with dense bidirectional attention, single shared noise level, conditioned on first-frame observations + per-agent actions. 10k iterations on 93-frame clips, then 6k on 189-frame clips. (2) Causal student: Diffusion Forcing formulation with block-causal + SHA, independently sampled noise per block. 15k iterations. (3) Conditional Self-Forcing distillation: DMD loss with rollout-aware training, 4-step denoising. Generator:critic update ratio 1:4. 400 iterations.

Multi-agent benchmark (Table 1): Γ-World outperforms Solaris and frame-concat baselines across all categories. FVD: 184.1 (Grounding) vs Solaris 333.8, 199.3 (Movement) vs 301.9, 191.5 (Building) vs 311.1, 264.5 (Consistency) vs 448.6. FID consistently best: 24.8 vs 51.7, 24.0 vs 36.1, 21.2 vs 36.3, 32.1 vs 71.0. Trained on 2-agent Minecraft episodes but generalizes to 4-agent zero-shot via simplex encoding.

Architecture ablation (Table 2): Sequence concat + Simplex + Sparse Hub achieves best overall (FVD=223.4, FID=30.2, PSNR=27.7). Progressive improvement: Spatial concat→Sequence concat (FVD 312.4→285.6), None encoding→View emb (285.6→256.3), View emb→Simplex (256.3→228.5), Full→Sparse Hub (228.5→223.4). Simplex encoding outperforms learned view embeddings by assigning distinct identities without privileged slot order matching agent exchangeability.

Efficiency of SHA (Figure 3): At 8 agents, self-attention FLOPs: dense ~17.6T vs sparse ~1.9T (9.3× reduction). DiT latency: dense 246ms vs sparse 129ms. At 2 agents: dense 2.0T vs sparse 1.9T FLOPs. Sparse Hub Attention provides practical scaling path—reduces cross-agent cost from quadratic to linear. Hub token ablation: e=8 tokens gives good balance; e=128 marginal improvement over e=32 (FVD 220.5 vs 221.8).

Distillation results (Table 5): Bidirectional teacher: FVD=227.3, Causal: FVD=266.4, Distilled: FVD=239.7. Distilled model recovers much of teacher quality while retaining causal streaming structure. Distillation effectively transfers bidirectional modeling capacity into streaming-compatible architecture. At inference: 4-step denoising, KV cache with rolling 24-frame local-attention window per view, 24 FPS streaming.

Zero-shot 4-player generalization: Model trained only on 2-agent data generates synchronized 4-agent rollouts without architecture changes or retraining. Enabled by: (1) Simplex encoding—additional agents activated by selecting unused simplex vertices from pool; (2) SHA—shared communication pathway without dense pairwise attention. Model maintains coupled multi-agent dynamics, not independent single-agent videos.

Action conditioning: Game domain uses 25-field action vector per agent per frame (23 discrete player controls + 2 continuous camera controls). Robot domain uses 10-field continuous vector (3D position + 6D rotation + gripper). Shared action encoder across all agents: two MLP branches lifting each modality to 128D, fusion MLP with 4×-stride 1D temporal convolution, projection to model dimension 2048. Action bias added as per-block bias to every transformer layer.

Limitations: Evaluation focuses primarily on gaming environments and robotics examples; broader validation in more complex, heterogeneous, long-horizon settings remains future work. Simplex pool supports agent-count scaling within fixed rotary agent band—very large populations may require larger bands or hierarchical grouping. Model does not explicitly enforce 3D geometry or physical constraints, so long rollouts may accumulate inconsistencies.

Interactive World Simulator: Action-conditioned video prediction framework for robot policy training and evaluation. Uses consistency models for both image decoding and latent-space dynamics prediction. Achieves stable video prediction for 10+ minutes at 15 FPS on single RTX 4090 GPU. Enables scalable data generation for imitation policy training (DP, ACT, π0, π0.5) and reproducible policy evaluation with strong sim-to-real correlation.

Two-stage architecture: Stage 1 autoencoder—CNN encoder maps RGB to 2D latent, consistency-model decoder (inspired by CTM) reconstructs from noisy inputs conditioned on latent. Training: same noise applied at two scales σt>σs≥0, decoder maps Dθ(xσt; σt,σs,z), loss L_AE=E[w(σt)·||x̂σs-xσs||²]. Stage 2 dynamics—freeze autoencoder, train action-conditioned consistency model Fψ as stack of 3D conv blocks with FiLM modulation and spatiotemporal attention. Only last frame receives full noise.

Key technique—noisy context injection: Small noise is injected into observation contexts during training so dynamics model Fψ becomes robust to noisy contexts. During online inference, model predictions serve as context for later steps and are inevitably noisy. This robustness is essential for stable long-horizon prediction (>10 minutes). Autoregressive inference shifts fixed-length context window, discarding old latents beyond threshold to bound computation cost.

Video prediction comparison (Table I): Aggregated across all tasks, outperforms Cosmos, UVA, DINO-WM, Dreamer4. MSE: 0.005±0.005 vs DINO-WM 0.028, Cosmos 0.019. PSNR: 25.82±2.72 vs Dreamer4 20.81, Cosmos 18.91. FVD: 243.20±103.58 vs Dreamer4 1747.26, Cosmos 799.34. Qualitative: baselines show robot pose drift, inaccurate object dynamics, missing details, severe artifacts over long rollouts. Model size only 176.02 MB for mug grasping task.

Data generation for policy training (Figure 5): Policies trained on 100% world simulator data perform comparably to 100% real data. DP: 87.9% (sim) vs 90.3% (real). ACT: 76.2% (sim) vs 73.6% (real). π0.5: 73.1% (100% sim) to 88.8% (100% real) with gradual improvement. Data scaling experiment (5-100 episodes): consistent improvement curves for both simulator and MuJoCo data, confirming comparable scaling behavior.

Sim-to-real policy evaluation correlation (Figure 7): Strong positive correlations across T pushing (r=0.8553), pile sweeping (r=0.8869), mug grasping (r=0.9908), rope routing (r=0.8455). Slight positive bias—policies tend to score slightly higher in simulator than real world. Error bars show Clopper-Pearson confidence intervals from 20 initial configurations per task. Demonstrates simulator utility for policy selection without real-world experiments.

Task diversity: 6 real-world tasks (Mug Grasping, Rope Routing, Rope Collecting, T Pushing, Box Packing, Pile Sweeping) involving rigid objects, deformable objects, articulated objects, object piles, and multi-object interactions. Uses ALOHA Bimanual Robot. ~600 episodes per task, 200 steps each, ~6 hours collection per task. Default image resolution 128×128. Training: ~6h (stage 1) + ~12h (stage 2) on single H200 GPU.

Teleoperation interface: Kinematic teleoperation device allows users to control robot actions through the world simulator in real time. Commands include move forward, open gripper, lift end-effector. Generates full demonstration trajectories {(ot,at)} directly compatible with standard IL pipelines (DP, ACT, π-series) without architecture modifications. World simulator data can be mixed with real demonstrations or used standalone.

Problem formulation: Given dataset D of episodes E={(o0,a0),...,(oT,aT)} where ot∈R^{3×H×W} is RGB image, build action-conditioned predictor ôt=f(ot-N:t-1, at-N:t-1) minimizing ||ôt-ot||². Autoregressive context window approach with fixed-length N for inference. Key design choices: (1) encode to compact 2D latent, (2) predict in latent space, (3) decode with consistency model, (4) noise injection for robustness.

Current limitations and future directions: Tested on moderate-sized datasets (~600 episodes per task); scaling behavior with larger interaction data and compute not yet studied. Sim-to-real gap exists (slight positive bias in simulator scores). Extension to more diverse environments and increasingly complex manipulation tasks planned. Understanding how world model performance scales with data/compute is identified as important future direction.

Hamiltonian World Models (HWM): A position paper proposing physically grounded generative world modeling through Hamiltonian dynamics. Argues the bottleneck of world models is not visual fidelity but physical meaningfulness and action usefulness. Classifies existing approaches into three routes: 2D video-generative (GAIA-1, Genie), 3D scene-centric (NeRF, Gaussian Splatting), and JEPA-like latent (I-JEPA, V-JEPA). Proposes Hamiltonian structure as a fourth paradigm with phase-space latents and energy-based dynamics.

Phase-space latent representation: Instead of unstructured latent vector, world model state zt=[qt,pt] where qt=generalized coordinates, pt=generalized momenta. This provides natural inductive bias—an agent must reason about object positions AND velocities. Motivated by HNN (Hamiltonian Neural Networks) and HGN (Hamiltonian Generative Networks). For multi-entity scenes: zt={qt^i,pt^i}_{i=1}^N, enabling object-centric relational structure.

Hamiltonian dynamics formulation: Given Hamiltonian H(q,p), evolution follows q̇=∂H/∂p, ṗ=-∂H/∂q. Equivalently ż=J∇_z H(z) with symplectic matrix J=[[0,I],[-I,0]]. The Hamiltonian decomposes: H_φ(zt)=ΣK_φ(p_t^i)+ΣU_φ(q_t^i)+ΣV_φ(q_t^i,q_t^j) (kinetic, potential, interaction terms). Model learns energy landscape rather than arbitrary vector field. Dynamics generated through gradients of learned energy function.

Controlled-dissipative extension: Real environments are not conservative, so HWM extends to ż=(J-R_ψ(zt))∇_z H_φ(zt)+G_ψ(zt)at+ε_ψ(zt,at), R_ψ(zt)≥0. Hamiltonian term=physical core, R_ψ=dissipative effects (friction, damping), G_ψ·at=action-conditioned control, ε_ψ=residual (contact discontinuities, unmodeled dynamics). Residual constrained via L_res=||ε_ψ(zt,at)||² to prevent degeneration into generic transition network.

Full HWM architecture pipeline: Eθ: observations→latent phase state; T_H: Hamiltonian-inspired dynamics rollout; Dθ: video generative decoder rendering predicted observations; U: utility/planning function. Pipeline: o≤t→(Eθ)→zt→(T_H with actions)→ẑt+1:t+H→(Dθ)→ôt+1:t+H→(U)→a*_t. Separates four roles: perception, dynamics, generation, planning. Physical evolution and visual rendering decoupled but coupled.

Planning interface: Given candidate action sequences {a^{(m)}}, model generates latent+visual rollouts: ẑt+1:t+H=T_H(zt,a^{(m)}), ôt+1:t+H~Dθ(ẑ). Best action selected: m*=argmax_m U(ẑ,ô,a^{(m)}), a*_t=a^{m*}_t. Aligned with visual foresight, MPC, and latent imagination. Difference: imagined futures grounded in structured phase-space rollout rather than unconstrained video predictor alone.

Critique of current paradigms: 2D video-generative models optimize perceptual realism more than physical consistency—may violate contact, momentum, action-effect consistency. 3D scene-centric models better suited to static reconstruction than action-conditioned dynamics. JEPA-like latent models predict abstract features but latent variables weakly tied to explicit physical quantities (momentum, energy, contact). All lack structure-preserving dynamics for long-horizon stability.

Key argument: Visual fidelity ≠ physical validity. Generated trajectory may look realistic over short horizon while gradually violating object permanence, contact consistency, momentum conservation, or action-effect causality. Generic learned dynamics z_{t+1}=f_θ(zt,at) doesn't specify what physical quantities latent state represents or what structure transition preserves. Leads to compounding errors, long-horizon drift, poor OOD generalization, high data requirements.

Five properties for embodied world models: (1) Physical grounding—respect geometry, contact, inertia, energy; (2) Data efficiency through physical priors—constrain hypothesis space; (3) Compositional structure—entity-level variables with relations; (4) Temporal consistency under rollout—stable multi-step prediction; (5) Causal action conditioning—answer counterfactual 'what if action a_t?' questions. Hamiltonian dynamics offers one principled way to satisfy all five.

Challenges: Real environments not ideal Hamiltonian (friction, control, impacts, dissipation, partial observability, non-rigid interactions). Inferring meaningful phase-space variables from pixels is nontrivial representation learning. Contact-rich manipulation, articulated systems, deformable objects may require hybrid/graph-based/event-driven extensions beyond smooth Hamiltonian flow. Current evaluation protocols cannot establish whether model learned reusable physical regularities vs task-specific correlations.

Future direction—physical world foundation models: Move from scene-level physical rollout to open-ended physical generalization. Future HWM should learn reusable physical mechanisms composable across unseen objects, materials, embodiments, tasks. Long-term goal: physically native foundation model capable of continual physical abstraction, composition, and self-evolution. May combine perceptual richness of 2D video generation, spatial grounding of 3D, compact abstraction of JEPA, and stability of energy-based phase-space dynamics.

CoWVLA (Chain-of-World VLA) establishes a new paradigm that unifies world-model temporal reasoning with disentangled latent motion representation, combining the compactness of latent actions with the temporal continuity and world knowledge of frame prediction

Latent motion extractor built on pretrained video VAE (VidTwin) explicitly disentangles each video segment into structure latent zs (global semantics, low-frequency dynamics) and two directional motion latents zm_h and zm_w (spatial averaging along height/width axes), concatenated into unified latent motion vector zm with Dm = f × dm × (hm + wm) = 1792

Two-stage training: (1) Pre-training where model takes instruction + initial frame, uses learnable motion query Q to predict latent motion ẑm and terminal frame vqf with causal masking preventing Q from attending to vqf; (2) Co-fine-tuning with alternating keyframe-action pairs [T, ṽq1, Q, Aq1, ṽq2, Aq2, ...] using single-Q-for-full-window design

VAE training objective: L_vae = L_rec + λ_p·L_p + λ_GAN·L_GAN + λ_KL·L_KL, combining reconstruction, perceptual, adversarial, and KL-divergence regularization losses to preserve temporal consistency and visual realism

Pre-training loss: L_pretrain = ||ẑm - zm||²₂ + Σ CE(v̂q_x, vq_x) for x∈{1,f}. First term enforces latent motion prediction accuracy, second term ensures terminal-frame visual consistency. Co-fine-tuning loss: L_finetune = Σ CE(Âq_j, Aq_j) + λ1||ẑm - zm(V1:f)||² + λ2 Σ CE(ṽ̂q_j, ṽq_j), balancing action prediction, latent motion supervision, and visual token prediction

CoWVLA achieves SOTA on LIBERO (0.956 avg) and SimplerEnv-WidowX (0.760 avg), outperforming both world-model approaches (UniVLA 0.950/0.687, FlowVLA 0.881/0.740) and latent-action approaches (Villa-X 0.901/0.625, TLA 0.952/0.480), demonstrating superior cross-domain robustness

Structure-motion disentanglement verified via cross-reconstruction: structure latent alone preserves global scene layout and object appearance while motion latent alone captures robot arm trajectories and fine-grained temporal dynamics. PCA clustering of motion latents reveals distinct motion patterns (monotonic rises, two-stage reversals, multi-phase back-and-forth)

Optimal hyperparameters are N=2 sparse keyframes and la=10 action chunk size (~20-frame/~2s temporal horizon). Inverted-U trend: too few keyframes under-constrain motion, too many allow short-term visual matching instead of dynamics inference; too small chunks reduce abstraction, too large introduce high future uncertainty

CoWVLA unifies three VLA pretraining paradigms: world-model VLA (predicts future frames but wastes capacity on redundant backgrounds), latent-action VLA (compact frame-to-frame transitions but lacks temporal continuity and world knowledge), and structure-motion disentangled video compression. The latent motion chain serves as carrier for perceiving essential dynamic changes while terminal keyframe prediction preserves visual landmarks

Latent motion space depends on quality and domain coverage of pretrained video VAE, potentially introducing distribution mismatch in new environments. Model relies on large 8.5B VLA backbone (Emu3) and substantial computational resources (32 A800 GPUs for pre-training)

X-WIN (X-ray World Intelligence Network) is a novel CXR world model that distills volumetric 3D knowledge from chest CT by learning to predict 2D projections in latent space under various 3D transformations (rotation of X-ray source). Core hypothesis: if a model can accurately predict how X-ray projections change under known spatial transformations, it has internalized meaningful 3D anatomical structure

Architecture uses JEPA-style dual-encoder: context encoder f_θ processes routine CXR projections, EMA encoder f_θ' receives target projections from CT volume. View predictor g_v conditioned on actions predicts novel projection representations: z_patch_i = g_v(Linear(a_i) ⊕ (f_θ(u_context) + PE)). Action a_i = k·Δφ defines relative yaw rotation angle, k steps of fixed rotation step Δφ

Affinity-guided contrastive alignment loss softens InfoNCE by incorporating an affinity matrix A that captures mutual similarities between projections from the same CT volume. L_align = L_InfoNCE + λ_affinity·L_affinity where A_ij = exp(sim(t_i,t_j)/τ̃)/Σ exp(sim(t_i,t_l)/τ̃). This addresses the limitation of hard alignment that disregards rich anatomical correspondences in projections from the same volume

Structure-preserving domain adaptation bridges simulated (CT projections from NLST) and real (CXR from MIMIC-CXR) domains via: (1) masked image modeling on both domains encoding local/contextual features, (2) domain classifier f_c trained with cross-entropy to differentiate domains, (3) L_domain = ||z_patch - t_patch||² - Σ log f_c(z_patch) enforcing predicted representations to be statistically similar to real domain while preserving structural information

Overall loss: L_overall = L_align + λ_MIM·L_MIM + λ_domain·L_domain + λ_cls·L_cls. Stop-gradient applied so L_domain does not update f_c. Optimal weights: λ_affinity=0.4, λ_domain=0.6, λ_MIM=1.0, λ_cls=1.0

X-WIN with ViT-Large achieves SOTA on linear probing across 5 CXR benchmarks: avg AUROC 0.892 (VinDr 0.925, CheXpert 0.908, NIH-CXR 0.843, RSNA 0.929, JSRT 0.857), outperforming CXR foundation models (Ark+ 0.863, CheXWorld 0.844) and vision-language models (MaCo 0.815). Few-shot fine-tuning on COVIDx achieves 0.993 AUROC

Action design ablation reveals: (1) direct yaw rotation outperforms step-wise rotation (0.925 vs 0.857 on VinDr) since step-wise diminishes degree of freedom by emphasizing transition dynamics between adjacent projections; (2) yaw-only rotation outperforms 3D Euler angle rotation (yaw+pitch+roll: 0.871) due to sufficient 3D spatial information and consistency with routine projections; (3) optimal rotation step size Δφ=3° provides best balance between projection density and target diversity

3D CT volume reconstruction demonstrates learned representations capture 3D information: VQ-GAN decoder on frozen encoder achieves 81.6% codebook usage, rendered 2D projections reach PSNR 30.23dB/SSIM 0.888, reconstructed 3D CT volumes achieve PSNR 27.87dB/SSIM 0.789, preserving overall structure with some blurriness from representation abstraction

X-WIN extends CheXWorld paradigm from 2D to 3D: CheXWorld learns 2D local structures, global geometry, and appearance variation using relative location of local crops as actions on CXRs. X-WIN instead internalizes 3D anatomical structure by predicting projections under 3D source rotations, distilling knowledge from CT. This 2D-to-3D leap underlies performance gains over CheXWorld (0.892 vs 0.844 avg AUROC)

Sim-to-real domain gap remains a challenge: domain adaptation loss improves cosine similarity from 0.845 to 0.967 and reduces L2 distance from 3.973 to 1.825, but reconstructed CT volumes show certain blurriness due to visual representation abstraction. Future work needed on invariant feature learning or domain mapping strategies for better data efficiency and generalizability

DriveWorld-VLA tightly integrates VLA and World Models at the representation level by using LLM hidden states as shared latent space for both future imagination and action prediction. Three coupling strategies compared: (a) Disentangled Interaction (WM as external simulator, limited knowledge transfer), (b) Feature-Sharing (shared representations but no action-conditioned causal reasoning), (c) Unified Feature Sharing & Causal Reasoning (DriveWorld-VLA's approach, optimizing WM latents as decision variables for what-if reasoning)

Feature-Level Sharing: multi-modal inputs (multi-view images, text, historical actions, BEV features) are independently tokenized and fed into InternVL3-2B VLM. Final-layer hidden states H_t serve as common feature space for both future BEV imagination (via denoiser) and action prediction (via action decoder). BEV features extracted by BEVFormer, flattened and projected as BEV tokens. Cross-attention produces B_t' = CROSSATTN_θ(B_t, H_t, H_t)

Three-Stage Progressive Training: Stage 1 (VLA & WM Joint Training) jointly learns future BEV imagination and action prediction with loss L_s1 = L_seg + L_act. Stage 2 (Action Controllability Fine-Tuning) conditions generative branch on future actions via flow-matching loss L_FM = ||DIT_θ(B_t', A_{t+Δt}, x_k, B'_{t+Δt}/k) - (B'_{t+Δt} - x_0)||². Stage 3 (Future-Guided Eval & Refinement) closes loop with reward-weighted action refinement L'_act = r̂·||A' - A||²

Action-conditioned flow-matching denoiser uses DiT architecture with two branches: (1) history-conditioned branch activated in Stage 1 for dense future supervision from historical observations, (2) future action-conditioned branch using DiT blocks to model controllable future evolution under different action sequences. Euler-based sampling with N=25 steps generates action-conditioned future imagination B'_{t+Δt}^{k+1} = B_{t+Δt}^k + (1/kN)·DIT_θ(B_t', A_{t+Δt}, x_k)

DriveWorld-VLA achieves SOTA: 91.3 PDMS on NAVSIMv1 (vs DiffusionDrive 88.1, WoTE 88.3, DriveVLA-W0 90.2), 86.8 EPDMS on NAVSIMv2 (vs DriveVLA-W0 86.1), and 0.16 avg collision rate on nuScenes (vs FSDrive 0.32, HERMES-p 0.17). NC=99.1 and EP=85.9 on NAVSIMv1 demonstrate effective safety constraint enforcement with efficient forward progress

Progressive training ablation reveals each stage incrementally improves NAVSIMv1 PDMS: Stage 1 alone 87.1, +Stage 2 gives +0.5 to 87.6, +Stage 3 gives +3.7 to 91.3. Non-progressive strategy (training Stage 2+3 simultaneously) achieves only 83.6 PDMS (-7.7 drop), demonstrating model must first learn from GT actions and enhance latent feature space before benefiting from action prediction. Feature-level supervision from denoising is critical—task-level supervision alone underperforms

Stage 1 loss: L_s1 = L_seg + L_act. Stage 2 loss: L_s2 = L_FM (flow-matching only). Stage 3 loss: L_s3 = L'_act + L_seg + L_rew, where L'_act = r̂_{t+Δt}·||A'_{t+Δt} - A_{t+Δt}||² (reward-weighted action loss), r̂_{t+Δt} = R(B'_{t+Δt}, B_{t+Δt}, A'_{t+Δt}) from learned reward model. Only reward model and action head are trained in Stage 3; denoiser and VLM are frozen

VLM strategy ablation shows optimal config: VLM pre-trained (following RecogDrive settings) + VLM parameters trained during Stage 1 (not frozen). Fully freezing VLM limits performance (DAC drops from 98.2 to 87.2), as VLM must undergo optimization to accurately model shared space features. Non-targeted pre-training alone insufficient—targeted VLM optimization needed to fully unlock potential

DriveWorld-VLA unifies generative imagination and proactive decision-making, advancing beyond HERMES (joint scene understanding/generation) and DriveVLA-W0 (future image prediction as self-supervised signal). Key distinction: WM latents serve as decision-making states enabling action-conditioned what-if reasoning through controllable imagination, transitioning from reactive planning to proactive optimization via counterfactual simulation in shared latent space

Stage 3 reward refinement shows minimal benefit for open-loop short-term planning (nuScenes 2nd/3rd second: -0.01/-0.02 CR change), as open-loop tasks do not necessarily benefit from generative supervision at all times. Reward Model has greater effect on closed-loop than open-loop planning. Different baselines and Reward Models across benchmarks may introduce inconsistencies. Progressive design requires sequential training adding complexity

World-VLA-Loop: a closed-loop framework that co-evolves a video world model and a VLA (Vision-Language-Action) policy through iterative refinement, using the world model as a virtual environment for RL post-training while feeding policy rollouts back to improve the world model.

Near-success failure modes in video world models: existing models like Cosmos-Predict 2 frequently hallucinate successful outcomes even under erroneous actions, particularly failing on near-success cases where minor action errors cause failure.

SANS (Success And Near-Success) dataset: combines successful trajectories and near-success failure trajectories (trajectories that nearly accomplish the goal but fail due to minor end-effector positioning errors) to force the world model to learn sharper success-failure boundaries in spatial dynamics.

State-aware video world model: built on Cosmos-Predict 2 with a reward prediction head (lightweight MLP) that maps diffusion denoised latents z_t to scalar rewards, jointly optimized with flow matching loss L = L_flow + lambda * sum(||r_hat_t - r_t||^2), where lambda is modulated by noise level per EDM framework.

Joint loss: L = L_flow + lambda * sum_{t=1}^{T} ||r_hat_t - r_t||^2, where L_flow is the original flow matching loss for video generation, the second term is reward prediction MSE, and lambda is modulated according to sampled noise level (EDM framework) for robustness against high-variance early denoising latents.

Closed-loop co-evolution: after each RL update, collect success and near-success rollouts from the updated VLA policy, augment SANS dataset, and fine-tune the world model. This iterative cycle keeps the world model aligned with the policy's evolving failure modes.

GRPO-based RL post-training pipeline: world model replaces physics simulator, autoregressively generating multi-step visual observations and binary reward predictions (threshold 0.9) conditioned on policy actions, used to compute group-relative advantages for policy updates.

World model achieves 88.5% average visual alignment and 87.25% average reward alignment across LIBERO and real-world tasks. RL post-training improves OpenVLA-OFT success rate by 12.7% on LIBERO and 23.4%/13.3% on two real-world tasks. Iterative refinement adds another 13.3% average improvement.

Ablation: removing reward prediction head drops visual alignment by ~30%, confirming joint reward-video training significantly improves the model's ability to discriminate between action outcomes. External VLM (Qwen3-VL) as reward judge underperforms the internal reward head due to hallucination.

LIBERO-100 (long-horizon tasks requiring >300 video frames) is left for future work because current autoregressive video models suffer severe quality drift over long sequences. The framework requires task-specific SANS data collection (~50 success + 50 failure trajectories per task).

Survey paper: first comprehensive review of efficiency techniques for video generation-based world models. Introduces a novel taxonomy across three dimensions: efficient modeling paradigms (diffusion distillation, AR/hybrid), efficient network architectures (VAE, memory, attention, RoPE), and efficient inference algorithms (parallelism, caching, pruning, quantization).

World model definition for video generation: learns the transition function P(s_{t+1}|s_t, a_t) where s represents video states (frames or latents) and a represents conditions or actions (text prompts, camera trajectories, or robot actions). The objective is to model causal mechanisms like gravity, collision, and object permanence rather than just pixel transitions.
