Diffusion model distillation for efficient sampling: three main approaches - (1) step-reduction distillation (e.g. GPD reduces Wan from 48 to 6 steps), (2) consistency distillation (e.g. VideoLCM, AnimateLCM enabling one-step generation), (3) adversarial distillation (e.g. DMD2, Seaweed-APT for real-time one-step video generation via distribution matching and GAN losses).

Hybrid AR-diffusion modeling: combines autoregressive temporal rollout with diffusion-based frame synthesis. Key works include Progressive Autoregressive Video Diffusion (progressive noise levels across frames), FramePack (compresses historical frames by importance), and streaming causal diffusion (CausVid, Self Forcing, Rolling Forcing) that causalizes diffusion for incremental frame generation.

Streaming causal diffusion variants: Self Forcing (conditions training on self-generated histories to bridge train-test gap), Rolling Forcing (rolling-window denoising for real-time multi-minute generation), Causal Forcing (ODE distillation from bidirectional teacher), Rolling Sink (training-free test-time cache maintenance for minute-scale generation), AAPT (adversarial post-training for streaming).

Efficient architectures for video world models: hierarchical/pyramidal generation (coarse-to-fine, e.g. Pyramidal Flow Matching, Waver cascade), deep VAE compression (DC-VideoGen: 64x spatial + 4x temporal; REGEN: generative decoder for ultra-compact semantic tokens), visual memory mechanisms (FramePack, WorldPack for long-term history compression), efficient attention (sparse, windowed, linear alternatives).

Long-horizon generation degradation: over a 10-minute generation window, all tested methods (Self Forcing, LongLive, Rolling Forcing, Deep Forcing, Causal Forcing, Rolling Sink) exhibit severe degradation in object permanence, structural consistency, and complex human-object interactions. Maintaining coherent 3D structures over minutes remains a significant unsolved challenge.

Three application domains for video world models: (1) Autonomous driving (GAIA series, Vista, Drive-WM for data synthesis, interactive simulation, and generative planning); (2) Embodied AI (DreamGen, DreamZero, UVA for data synthesis, interactive simulation, and world action models); (3) Game/interactive simulation (GameGen-X, Matrix-Game 2.0, DreamerV4 for action-conditioned generation and RL).

Convergence trend: 'generate-evaluate-retrain' loop emerging in autonomous driving and embodied AI, where closed-loop interaction continuously produces new samples and hard cases, shifting data provisioning from offline augmentation to online iteration. Representative integrative works (Genie Envisioner, Cosmos, LingBot) combine data generation, interactive simulation, feedback, and policy optimization.

Key efficiency bottlenecks for video world models: autoregressive models face KV cache explosion during long-sequence generation, diffusion models suffer high latency from iterative denoising, vast redundancy in video frames must be reduced without losing semantics, and spatiotemporal modeling has cubic complexity growth. Efficiency is a fundamental prerequisite for real-time deployment.

World Action Models (WAMs): embodied foundation models that unify predictive state modeling with action generation, targeting joint distribution p(o', a | o, l) over future states and actions rather than actions alone. Two core criteria: (1) forward predictive modeling of future states, (2) coupled action generation strictly aligned with anticipated future states.

Formal definitions: VLA minimizes -log p(a|o,l) (action only), WM minimizes -log p(o'|o,a) (state transition only), WAM minimizes -log p(o',a|o,l) (joint future states and actions). WAMs generalize both VLA and WM by jointly modeling state evolution and motor control.

Cascaded WAM: sequential two-stage pipeline that first predicts next state (pixel, latent, or flow space) then derives actions. Factorizes p(o',a|o,l) = p(a|o',o,l) * p(o'|o,l). World model and action decoder are optimized as distinct modules. Two subtypes: Explicit Planning (pixel-space) and Implicit Planning (latent-space).

Explicit Cascaded WAM methods: UniPi (text-conditioned U-Net video + CNN inverse dynamics model), VLP (VLM for hierarchical sub-action generation + tree-search value scoring), RoboEnvision (non-autoregressive keyframe + interpolation), AVDC (optical flow extraction for geometric action decoding), Veo-Act (multi-head IDM + reactive VLA for contact-rich phases), pi0.7 (world model generates subgoal images injected into VLA context).

Implicit Cascaded WAM methods: VPP (VAE-encoded latent prediction + lightweight policy for real-time control), S-VAM (self-distillation from multi-step SVD teacher to single-pass), mimic-video (flow matching + partial denoising at intermediate ODE checkpoint), MWM (future semantic mask latents instead of RGB), LAPA (unsupervised latent action pretraining via VQ-VAE from unlabeled video).

Joint WAM: unified model predicting future world states and actions simultaneously with joint training. Two generation routes: (1) Autoregressive Generation (serialize states and actions into token space with causal left-to-right decoding - e.g. GR-1, GR-2, CoTVLA, WorldVLA, VLA-JEPA), and (2) Diffusion-based Non-Autoregressive Generation (joint refinement through parallel denoising - e.g. DreamZero, UVA, UWM, CosmosPolicy).

WAM vs related concepts: VAMs (Video Action Models) are WAMs limited to video modality; Video Policies use video backbones for representations but don't require active state prediction commitment; Action World Models (AWMs) are functionally similar but position the system as augmented simulator vs WAM positioning it as primary Agent. WAM is the broader, backbone-agnostic superset.

World models for VLA serve four roles: (a) Imitation learning augmentation - generate/filter training trajectories; (b) Model-based RL - imagined rollouts as surrogate environments; (c) Reward modeling - derive rewards from learned dynamics (VIPER, Diffusion Reward, SRPO); (d) Policy evaluation - data-driven simulators replacing costly physical testing (Ctrl-World, Veo Robots).

WAM data ecosystem: four sources - (1) Robot teleoperation (OXE, DROID, RoboMIND, AgiBot World); (2) Portable human demonstrations (UMI-style, DexUMI for dexterous); (3) Simulation data (ManiSkill, RoboCasa, CALVIN, LIBERO); (4) Internet-scale egocentric video (Ego4D, EPIC-KITCHENS, HowTo100M). Internet video enables pretraining without action annotations via latent action inference.

WAM evaluation taxonomy organized around three axes: (1) Visual fidelity (PSNR, SSIM, LPIPS, FVD for world model generation quality); (2) Physical commonsense (VideoPhy, PhyGenBench, WorldScore for physics plausibility); (3) Action plausibility (MetaWorld, RLBench, CALVIN, LIBERO, RoboTwin for real/sim robot task success). Current protocols lack integrated assessment of all WAM-relevant capabilities.

Matrix-Game 3.0: a memory-augmented interactive world model achieving 720p real-time generation at up to 40 FPS with a 5B model, while maintaining stable memory consistency over minute-long sequences. Four key components: error-aware interactive base model, camera-aware long-horizon memory, training-inference aligned few-step distillation, and real-time inference acceleration.

Error-aware base model training: maintains an error buffer E during training. Error collection computes residuals delta = x_hat_i - x_i (difference between clean estimate and ground truth). Error injection samples delta ~ Uniform(E) and perturbs history latents x_tilde_i = x_i + gamma*delta, simulating imperfect contexts from autoregressive rollout. This enables self-correction during long-horizon streaming inference.

Camera-aware long-horizon memory: retrieves memory frames based on camera pose and field-of-view overlap using Plücker-style relative encoding. Memory latents, recent past latents, and current noised latents are placed in the same self-attention space (unified DiT framework) rather than as a separate memory branch. Optionally retains first latent as persistent 'sink latent' for global scene anchor.

Head-wise perturbed RoPE: for the h-th attention head, effective rotary base theta_hat_h = theta_base * (1 + sigma_theta * epsilon_h), where epsilon_h is head-dependent. This breaks periodic synchronization across heads, mitigates positional aliasing between distant memory and current frames, and discourages overly literal copying from temporally distant memory while preserving useful long-range access.

Multi-segment DMD distillation: bidirectional student performs multi-segment rollouts during training to mimic actual few-step inference. Each segment starts from random noise, past frames from previous segment tail, memory from online-updated pool. DMD objective minimizes reverse KL divergence between data and student distributions, approximated by difference of two score functions. Cold-start with single-segment + GT past frames (600 steps), then multi-segment (2400 steps).

Real-time inference acceleration: (1) INT8 quantization for DiT attention projections; (2) MG-LightVAE with 75% pruning ratio achieving 5.2x decoding speedup; (3) GPU-based camera-aware memory retrieval using sampling-based frustum overlap approximation (s_approx instead of exact s_exact). Asynchronous deployment with 8 GPUs for DiT + 1 GPU for VAE decoding achieves 40 FPS at 720p.

Industrial-scale data engine: three sources - (1) Unreal-Gen pipeline with 1000+ UE5 scenes, tick-synchronized capture of Video-Pose-Action-Prompt quadruplets, 10^8+ character variants; (2) Four-layer AAA game recording system (GTA V, RDR2, Cyberpunk 2077) with NavMesh-based autonomous exploration and physics-based WSAD action inference; (3) Real-world datasets (DL3DV-10K, RealEstate10K, OmniWorld, SpatialVid). Quality filtering removes 20% of raw data.

GPU-based memory retrieval is the most critical acceleration component: removing it causes the largest FPS drop (from ~40 to 6.60 FPS). INT8 quantization provides 12.62 FPS gain, MG-LightVAE provides 14.21 FPS gain. The 28B MoE model further improves generation quality, dynamics, and generalization through noise-regime decoupling (action control in high-noise model, visual refinement in low-noise model).

Viewpoint-specialized scaling for 28B model: trains separate high-noise models for first-person and third-person views (shared common low-noise model). Action control trained on action-accurate data in narrow noise regime; visual quality refined using Internet video data in low-noise model. Progressive resolution and clip length scaling for stable long-horizon behavior.

Prior interactive world models face a fundamental trade-off: Matrix-Game 2.0 and HY-World achieve real-time streaming but lack memory for minute-long consistency; LingBot-World extends context length for geometric consistency but struggles with real-time deployment. RELIC's camera-aware KV cache introduces computational overhead limiting scalability. No existing open method simultaneously achieves long-horizon memory consistency, high-resolution fidelity, and real-time interaction.
